description: This figure presents electron backscatter diffraction (EBSD) characterization of microstructural evolution in a 30Mn-13Al-1.3C-5Cr duplex low-density steel subjected to various annealing treatments. The upper row (a1-f1) displays phase mappings (PH) distinguishing FCC austenite (cyan) and BCC ferrite (red), while the lower row (a2-f2) shows grain-reference-orientation-deviation (GROD) mappings of austenite with dark lines indicating high-angle grain boundaries (>15°). The six columns represent: cold-rolled condition (CR), annealing at 950°C for 1 min (DP950-1) and 5 min (DP950-5), 1000°C for 1 min (DP1000-1) and 5 min (DP1000-5), and 1050°C for 1 min (DP1050-1). Key observations reveal that CR exhibits shear bands in deformed austenite. DP950-5 shows the highest ferrite fraction (~36%) with coarse granular α and banded δ ferrite, partial recrystallization (54.8%), and remaining shear bands. Increasing annealing temperature to 1000°C and above (DP1000-1, DP1000-5, DP1050-1) promotes complete recrystallization with annealing twin formation, reduced ferrite content, and grain growth from 5.13±4.74 μm (DP1000-1) to 9.65±8.17 μm (DP1050-1). The GROD maps indicate that yellow regions (0°-2° misorientation) correspond to recrystallized grains, while red/pink regions (2°-180°) represent deformed structures. The figure demonstrates that annealing at 1000°C achieves optimal microstructure with fully recrystallized austenite matrix, fine dispersed ferrite bands, and minimized carbides, supporting the superior strength-ductility combination reported for DP1000-5.
